questions.question: Why must a participant in the 2PC protocol write a <ready T> record to its local log before voting 'yes'?
expectedPoints: The record provides durability for the participant's vote.
If the participant crashes after voting, upon recovery it can check the log to see it had promised to commit.
This record forces the participant to remain bound to the protocol's outcome and cannot unilaterally abort.

questions.question: Describe the steps of the recovery process for a distributed database system, explaining the purpose of each major phase.
expectedPoints: The process begins by analyzing the log to find the last checkpoint and identify active transactions.
The Redo Phase: Reapplies all operations of committed transactions from the log. This ensures durability for commits that may not have been written to disk.
The Undo Phase: Rolls back (undos) all operations of transactions that were active (not committed) at the time of the crash. This ensures atomicity ('all or nothing').
Integration with 2PC: For participants in a 'ready' state, recovery involves querying the coordinator or other participants to discover the global decision.
The use of stable storage for the log is critical to ensure records survive a crash.
